title: Revision of the Maritime Spatial Planning Directive/the Ocean Act
rationale: The Maritime Spatial Planning Directive revision (part of the EU Ocean Act) governs how member states zone and plan uses of marine space — fisheries, shipping, energy, conservation. While there could be marginal animal welfare or climate angles, the directive is fundamentally a spatial-planning instrument and EA-aligned cause areas are not substantively engaged. Without further specifics suggesting welfare or catastrophic-risk provisions, this falls in 'other'.

scope.note: Affects EU marine spatial planning across member states but is a procedural/coordination instrument with diffuse welfare impact.

reversibility.note: Standard directive revision, revisable through normal legislative cycles.

tractability.note: Early-stage call for evidence with deadline well in the future, framing still movable.

urgency.note: Deadline ~8 months out (2026-07-16); no time pressure.

counterfactual.note: Marine NGOs and industry will respond heavily; EA-aligned marginal value is low absent a specific welfare hook.

title: Public Consultation for the EU Youth Strategy post-2027
summary: The 2019-2027 EU Youth Strategy is coming to an end. The strategy supports social and civic engagement for young people. It also aims to improve policy decisions and their impact on young people. The aim of this public consultation is to gather views and suggestions to inform the European Commission’s proposals for the post-2027 EU Youth Strategy.
rationale: This consultation concerns the EU's general youth strategy on civic engagement and policy participation, which does not substantively map onto any EA cause area. While youth wellbeing is broadly relevant, the strategy is a generic policy framework rather than a mental-health or wellbeing-metrics initiative. EA-aligned engagement here would be low-leverage relative to other EU consultations.

scope.note: Affects EU youth policy coordination but is largely a soft framework with limited binding reach across ~80 million young Europeans.

reversibility.note: Strategies run on multi-year cycles and are routinely revisited every 7-8 years.

tractability.note: Early-stage pre-formulation consultation seeking input to shape Commission proposals, so framing is genuinely open.

urgency.note: Deadline is roughly 8 months out, well beyond the actionable window.

counterfactual.note: Youth NGOs and civil society organisations will respond in large numbers, leaving little marginal value for an additional EA submission.

title: Public consultation on Evaluation of Council Directive 2011/70/Euratom and Council Directive 2006/117/Euratom
rationale: This consultation evaluates two Euratom directives on the management of spent fuel/radioactive waste and shipments of radioactive waste — a technical nuclear-safety and waste-management framework. It does not substantively address nuclear weapons, proliferation, or catastrophic risk, and is best classified as 'other'. Civil radioactive-waste governance is important but outside the EA priority cause areas as defined here.

scope.note: Affects radioactive waste management practices across EU member states — meaningful but a narrow technical domain with limited direct welfare impact.

reversibility.note: Standard directive evaluation cycle; findings feed into a revisable legislative review process.

tractability.note: Evaluation-stage consultation, so framing of problems and gaps is still genuinely open to input.

urgency.note: Deadline ~7-8 months out, leaving ample time and little immediate pressure.

counterfactual.note: Nuclear industry, regulators, and established NGOs routinely engage on Euratom waste directives; marginal value of an EA-aligned submission is low.

title: Public consultation on the energy efficiency framework for the decade ahead
rationale: This consultation concerns the EU's energy efficiency framework for the next decade, a core climate mitigation policy lever affecting buildings, industry, and appliances across the EU. While the topic is important and has long-term implications, energy efficiency policy is heavily covered by mainstream environmental NGOs and industry, limiting the marginal value of an additional EA-aligned submission.

scope.note: A decade-long EU-wide energy efficiency framework affects hundreds of millions of people and substantial GHG emissions trajectories.

reversibility.note: Framework regulations of this kind set durable direction but are revisable through subsequent reviews and recasts.

tractability.note: Early-stage consultation on the next decade's framework — overall framing and ambition level are still genuinely movable.

urgency.note: Deadline is roughly 6+ months out (June 2026), so there is no near-term pressure to respond.

counterfactual.note: Energy efficiency is a saturated EU policy area with extensive coverage by climate NGOs, industry, and member states.

title: Public consultation on the renewable energy framework for the decade ahead
rationale: This is a foundational EU consultation on the renewable energy framework for the next decade, directly relevant to climate change mitigation through grid decarbonisation and renewables deployment. While the topic is important and has multi-year lock-in, mainstream climate and energy NGOs as well as industry will respond heavily, reducing the counterfactual value of an EA-aligned submission.

scope.note: A decade-long renewable energy framework affects EU-wide emissions trajectories and energy systems for hundreds of millions of people.

reversibility.note: A 10-year framework locks in investment signals and infrastructure decisions that are hard to reverse mid-cycle.

tractability.note: Early-stage public consultation on the framework's direction, so framing and priorities are still genuinely movable.

urgency.note: Deadline is roughly 6+ months away (June 2026), giving ample time but no immediate pressure.

counterfactual.note: Mainstream climate and energy NGOs plus industry will saturate this consultation, leaving little marginal value for an additional EA-aligned response.

title: Electronic invoicing – revision of EU rules
rationale: This consultation concerns the revision of EU rules on electronic invoicing, a technical single-market and tax-administration matter with no substantive connection to any EA cause area. It is the kind of routine business-process regulation that falls squarely under 'other'.

scope.note: Affects businesses and public authorities across the EU using e-invoicing, but the welfare impact per affected entity is small and indirect.

reversibility.note: Standard regulatory revision; can be revisited in normal legislative cycles.

tractability.note: Mid-stage revision where technical provisions remain open, though EA-relevant levers are essentially absent.

urgency.note: Deadline is roughly 6 months away, leaving ample time but no immediate pressure.

counterfactual.note: Industry, tax practitioners, and accounting bodies will heavily dominate this consultation; an EA-aligned submission adds essentially no marginal value.

title: Open Public Consultation on the Update of the Rules on Shareholder Rights
rationale: This consultation concerns updating EU corporate governance rules on shareholder rights — a technical area of company law and capital markets policy with no substantive overlap with EA cause areas. While shareholder engagement can sometimes affect ESG or animal welfare outcomes indirectly, the consultation framing is general corporate governance, which falls under 'other'.

scope.note: Affects listed companies and their shareholders across the EU — meaningful but a defined corporate-governance population.

reversibility.note: Standard directive update, revisable through normal legislative cycles every several years.

tractability.note: Open public consultation at an early stage on rule update; framing and scope still movable.

urgency.note: Deadline roughly 5-6 months away, leaving ample time with no immediate pressure.

counterfactual.note: Corporate governance consultations attract heavy response from industry, investor associations, and civil-society groups; EA-aligned input is not the relevant gap here.

title: Audiovisual media services – evaluation and update of EU rules
rationale: This consultation concerns the EU's Audiovisual Media Services Directive — a sectoral media-regulation framework covering broadcasting, video-on-demand, and online video platforms. It does not substantively engage any EA cause area; while AI-generated content or election integrity could be peripheral, the core subject is media policy. Marginal EA value of responding is low.

scope.note: AVMSD governs media services across the EU affecting hundreds of millions of viewers, but the welfare stakes per person are modest.

reversibility.note: Standard directive evaluation cycle; the AVMSD has been revised before and will be revisable again.

tractability.note: This is an early-stage evaluation/call-for-evidence on whether and how to update the rules, so framing is still open.

urgency.note: Deadline is roughly 5-6 months out, leaving ample time and little pressure to act now.

counterfactual.note: Media-policy consultations attract heavy engagement from broadcasters, platforms, and civil-society media NGOs; an EA-aligned submission adds little marginal value.

title: Revision of national targets and flexibilities in the EU climate policy framework after 2030
rationale: This consultation revises national emissions targets and flexibility mechanisms in the EU's post-2030 climate framework, a foundational decision shaping member-state decarbonisation pathways for the next decade. While the topic is highly important, climate policy is a saturated advocacy space with many well-resourced NGOs already engaged, lowering the marginal counterfactual value of an EA-aligned submission. The deadline is several months out, giving ample time to prepare a quality response.

scope.note: Sets binding national targets and flexibility rules affecting emissions across all 27 member states for the post-2030 period.

reversibility.note: Post-2030 framework will be revisable in subsequent climate packages but carries substantial inertia and locks in member-state planning.

tractability.note: Early-stage consultation on the architecture of national targets and flexibilities — framing and design choices are still genuinely open.

urgency.note: Deadline is roughly 5-6 months out, leaving plenty of time but no immediate pressure.

counterfactual.note: Mainstream climate NGOs and industry will respond heavily; an additional EA-aligned submission has limited marginal value.

title: International Carbon Credits
rationale: This initiative concerns the EU's framework for international carbon credits, likely tied to meeting climate targets through credits from outside the EU. It falls squarely within climate change mitigation policy, addressing how offsets and international credits integrate with EU emissions accounting. Climate is a well-resourced advocacy space, so EA marginal value is modest.

scope.note: International carbon credit rules affect EU-wide climate accounting and potentially significant emission flows, but their real-world climate impact depends heavily on integrity rules.

reversibility.note: Carbon credit frameworks tend to set durable rules of the game for offset markets but are revisable through periodic climate-policy reviews.

tractability.note: Appears to be an early-stage initiative on whether and how to integrate international credits, with framing still genuinely open.

urgency.note: Deadline is roughly 5+ months out, providing ample time before action is required.

counterfactual.note: Mainstream climate NGOs and industry groups (e.g., Carbon Market Watch) reliably engage on carbon credit consultations, limiting marginal EA value.

title: Open Public consultation on a Construction Services Act proposal
rationale: This is an EU consultation on a Construction Services Act, addressing single-market regulation of the construction sector. It does not substantively touch any EA-priority cause area such as AI governance, animal welfare, global health, or catastrophic risks.

scope.note: Sector-specific rule affecting construction services across the EU single market, with limited direct welfare impact.

reversibility.note: Standard EU regulation, revisable through normal legislative review cycles.

tractability.note: Open public consultation at proposal stage suggests framing is still movable.

urgency.note: Deadline approximately 6 months away (May 2026), allowing ample time but little pressure.

counterfactual.note: Construction industry stakeholders will dominate, but this is not an EA-relevant gap worth filling.
